speaker: interviewer
text: --memories of Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever, while in Auschwitz, encounter any infamous individuals?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I told you I saw Mengele. You know, I knew who he was. I saw him. I saw him walk into the block where he worked. And I saw him on the platform, from the back, never from the front. But I never talked to him, or anything of that. And the other one, Kramer, I saw. Two or three times he came to the block where the children were, and they would call him Papa Kramer, for some reason. Sometimes he would bring them candy. He-- he looked like a benevolent grandfather, which he was not. And then I saw him again-- I don't think I saw him in Belsen. But people were saying that the commandant of Belsen is Kramer.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you remember Kramer looking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sort of roly poly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fair, dark?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gray. Maybe he wasn't roly poly. Maybe he was, like, a heavyset man. I have no idea. You know, those are things-- that is really [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] too much to ask after 50 years, or 55 years. I have no idea. I know he was-- he was not a tall man, and he was a heavyset man, and it seemed to me he was grayish. For some reason the children called him Papa, so he must have looked in a grandfatherly way. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then we were to [INAUDIBLE], so we-- we went with Ruth. She must have the-- she-- she must have a number. I think she has-- I have 601, and she has 603. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this [? Helena ?] told us both, if we needed anything, she-- we should try to find her. She told us the block she was on. She was working in a-- in the women's camp in a-- in the registry, [? Striebstuber ?], in the general-- main registry.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went through this, after the-- at least we did not see, this time, the gassing of the children and of the people. We went through the selection, we were told-- which was in the children's block. The chil-- it was emptied on the-- on the chimney. We had to undress and march naked in front of the assembled [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] of Auschwitz. The chief was-- that's a good name, not only this [? Kramer ?] but [? Schwartzhuber ?]. That was our commandant. Maybe I should-- Should I spell it? No. So he was the-- and they-- they were drunk, terribly drunk. There were about five of them. We marched on the chimney, and they would shoot left to right. My mother didn't make it. We tried. I tried.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- we found somebody to lend me some lipstick, or I had a piece of lipstick, and tried to rouge her cheeks and such. But it didn't-- and my mother-in-law didn't make it, but that's another story. She-- she went with another selection when my mother-in-law made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was all this happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was all the end of June, 1944. And they, literally, that same day, that same Sunday-- it was very hot-- marched us with some clothes on, not without clothes this time. No, we got clothes. We got special clothes. We got bloomers that were blue and white striped-- sort of-- not bloomers. Sort of long underpants.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we got overalls that were made out of, like, flour sack material, you know? With short sleeves. And then we got shoes, not very different from this, but high. And they marched us into the Frauenlager, and there was an orchestra playing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they played [? 'Granada' ?] and [? 'Ramona.' ?] I can't-- when I hear it today, I-- that I can't listen to. Anyhow. And we came to this Frauenlager, to this woman's camp. And that was a lulu, because these women, they were Polish, and Ukrainian, Jewish, non-Jewish, everything mixed together. Never mind. But the Poles and the Ukrainians hate the Jews, and they hate each other. And they were there for three, four years already, at that point. Or three years. Camp was since 1940, '41. And they were hardened ladies. Most of them were lesbians. And they were a pack-- oh, oh, oh. They were really-- they were bad. And the SS women were-- they walking with the whips. And it was awful. That was, you know, the few days in this women's camp was really an eye opener. And then then, again, you know, it goes always like wires through the camp-- a transport is leaving Auschwitz. We were there. We didn't know what we were doing. So they read out the names who are leaving Auschwitz that afternoon.

speaker: interviewee
text: And neither Ruth nor I were in it. Now, next day-- next day, they should be assembled. And we were not in it. And we were desperate. We said, so, we can't stay here. I mean, maybe they won't send another transport. What are we going to do? So we got up at 4:00 in the morning. It was very bright, like today, end of June, beginning of July, I don't know. Very hot. And that was against all the rules. You know? You cannot walk around the camp at 4:00 in the morning because the camp starts functioning at 4:30, or whatever. And we remembered where this [? Helenka ?] told us where to find her, and we found her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we said we had to get out. We have to be in this transport. Please arrange it. And she did. She changed us for two other people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we went. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we left Auschwitz, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] which we never thought we would, alive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And on this big gate, there was a clock, and I think it said 2:30. I don't know what it said. And the wagons were not locked. They were only 40 of us. And there were two soldiers sitting on the steps. And there were two pails-- two pails with water, and a pail for us, or two pails each. And-- and we were-- the train went through a normal countryside, and there was-- and people were working in the fields, and there were flowers. And we just couldn't understand that the world is going on, that there is not Auschwitz everywhere, you know. It was [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Anyhow. And the train went, and went, and then, in the middle of the night, it stopped. And there was a-- an air raid. And after the air raid was over-- that doesn't-- that didn't bother us. And after the air raid was over, so the train went on. And the soldiers were very nice, I must say, for German soldiers. They emptied our pails because we were not allowed to get out. And they put fresh lime into it. And they brought water when we stopped at the station. They didn't steal our rations. We each got a small loaf of breath for the journey. But they wouldn't tell us where we are going. And we just went, and went, and went. I think it took about five days. In the middle of the war, they transported labor. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we-- we had no idea until, all of a sudden, you saw-- the first time we saw Magdeburg. So we knew we were going north. We were in Germany, middle of Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we-- then there is-- was a sign. It was summer, so that made everything much easier. There was a sign-- Harburg-Hamburg, hyphenated. We said, we-- we must be crazy, you know? Auschwitz is down here. Hamburg is down there-- up there. Anyway. So they brought us again to a siding. And there were masses of young men. There was a barrack. There was a siding, like-- not-- not a barrack. It-- sort of a row of houses, it looked. And in every window, there was a young man, and they were cheering, you know? They-- they-- they were Italian prisoners of war. So we disembarked, and we were put into tobacco warehouses. And we were over the river. And there, we had bunks. We were two to a bunk, and we had blankets. And there was-- there were flushing toilets, about 20 of them. And there were faucets with water, which ran.

speaker: interviewee
text: But 20 toilets for 500 people is not that much. So within two days, everything was again blocked and yucky. But it was there. The next morning, they sort of-- they got us up at 3:30, and that was our waking up time from then on until the end of the war, until we got to Belsen. 3:30 every morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in some ways, it was all right, you know? It was light, and we got, again, some sort of a coffee or whatever. The ersatz, and the piece of bread, and margarine, and-- and then they counted us and we marched-- [INAUDIBLE] And on the river-- it was the Elbe-- they put us on a boat.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were on the river for about, I don't know, half an hour, 20 minutes. And then we disembarked again, and they marched us to a place where there was a huge sign [? Rennania ?] Shell Oil Company. And they-- we were pushing lorries. It was sort of wagons with debris, which was quite hard, quite difficult because they were on rails again. And then we met the French prisoners of war, and that was wonderful. They really helped us. My god, did they help us. And they-- they shared everything with us. They bribed the guards, their-- our guards, and their guards, and they had Red Cross parcels. They gave us-- They shared everything. They equipped us for winter. They gave us sweaters, and socks, and long underwear, and toothpaste, and sugar, and what have you. They were very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the camp in Hamburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was Hamburg. I don't know. And two months, Hamburg. In Hamburg, and-- it was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the harbor. It was at the-- there was no other name. And then we were a satellite camp-- camp of [INAUDIBLE] That we were, that I know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But after that, in, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I guess it was in September, they moved us to a suburb-- whatever, suburb-- a little out of Hamburg to a place called Neugraben. Should I spell it for you? Because we were there for quite a while. It's capital N-E-U-G-R-A-B-E-N. I don't think I ever gave you that name. Or did I? There we-- there we were building houses for-- prefab houses for people who were-- who lost everything through bombardment. And funnily enough, I have a friend in London. And when she heard this story, she-- she flew to Hamburg, and she went to look into Neugraben. And the houses are standing, and people are living in them. That was five years ago, six years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and from, when we finished this, so we were again taken by train early in the morning, and there was snow on the ground, which was very unpleasant. And we had shoes that were wooden-- that had wooden soles. And they were like skis when the snow was picky. So we were walking like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went by train to-- to a suburb of Hamburg. It doesn't matter what it was called, [? Williamsburg ?], but it's-- what's the difference? And we were cleaning bricks on the street from the bombardments. And we were feeding them into a machine, special machine which spilt on the other side brand new bricks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and there, Jewish houses-- Jewish-- German Hausfraus saw us, and they spit on us walking their children to school and said, look at them, the dirty Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We are suffering because of them in the great big war.

speaker: interviewee
text: So nobody will ever tell me that the Germans didn't know. We all-- we were there for everybody to see, plain and simple. So that was that. And one day, we-- they-- that was-- we were-- and Hamburg had an added attraction while we were in Hamburg. We survived 240 air raids.

speaker: interviewee
text: During the day, at night, everywhere. We-- we always thought it-- they won't touch us, and that it's not meant for us, and nothing can happen to us. But one day, there was a bomb which fell by mistake into the camp and-- during the day, and killed seven girls. But that was-- basically, at night, they locked us up and went. And during the day, they had to take us with them to the-- to the bunkers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had no shelter during the air raids at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. But we didn't mind. But they locked us. That was unpleasant. But during the day, we were always in a shelter because our guards had to be in a shelter. So they had to take us with them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Eva, what was the most difficult thing to cope with in Hamburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe the cold, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after the summer. The cold. To keep yourself clean. Not to give in, you know? We made a vow that, no matter what, we'll-- how tired, how cold, how miserable, we'll still go to the washroom every night. And we'll wash in the cold water. So we did. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So that was-- that was tough. And hunger, because there was nobody to feed us. After we parted with the French, we had nobody. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that lasted only two months, maybe. Maybe three months. July, August, Sept-- middle September. But after that, it was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the daily rations like that you did receive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, minuscule. Sometimes we were lucky because where we worked, the German supervisor-- not the SS who supervised us, but the German foreman, you could say, you know, they would-- they got rations from a-- from a central kitchen. And those were wonderful soups, mostly pea soup. And-- and if we were a small group, and if it was a good foreman, so he would leave some of it for us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But there were only-- there was one foreman. I see him in front of me-- sort of a German, not like you imagine German to look. And he was from Buxtehude. And he always left us some, a little bit in the barrel. And he-- he didn't let the SS women take it away from us. So that-- he was nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I have no idea what he was called. No way. Do you think he talked to us? And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any opportunities to get your hands on food any other way?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, not there. Once we-- That's a long story. [INAUDIBLE] Once, we got a hold of some food after bombardment. With a great risk to us, we got hold of some sugar, and jam, and bread. But that was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that didn't sustain us, no. We were hungry. We were basically hungry, very hungry. The rations were a little bigger than in Auschwitz, but not much bigger.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were doing physical labor every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only physical labor. Only physical labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever receive any mail?

speaker: interviewee
text: I once-- that's-- that's the story from the book. Yes, I received a letter from out of the blue from--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it exceptional to receive mail?

speaker: interviewee
text: You didn't receive mail. That was a big secret. The girl who slept above me in the bunk gave me, one day, in the evening, a piece of paper and said, look at it. And if it's [? Rudolf's ?] handwriting, you can answer it. But don't ask me how I got it. And if-- and this was January, '45. And if you have an address in Prague, where he can find it, so put it there. And she gave me a pencil, because I didn't-- and a paper. And her name was Irene. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know her last name.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I was thinking, should I do this? Because I-- I put these people in Prague into jeopardy. And then I said to myself, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they'll give it to them only if the war is practically over. Otherwise, what can I do? So I wrote a short note saying that I was alive and in Hamburg, and it was January '45, and I was still well. And I got his note. And that's what he found when he got to Prague. That's how he came to look for me in Hamburg.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was a big secret. Nobody else got the letter. Nobody-- I mean, I wasn't allowed to say. Maybe somebody else did get something. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eva, when did you leave Hamburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: We left-- we moved to another place, but that doesn't matter. It was-- it was-- it was stupid, that other-- the last place. It was in the middle of a factory. It was not stupid. It was bad. Bad accommodations, as accommodations go. And it must have been the end of February, or the first week in March. I-- I can't pin it down. I really can't. Because [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know. That's-- that's when it was. It was certainly not earlier and not later. It was around the first of March. And they loaded us. They counted us, loaded us into cattle wagons-- this time 120 to a-- a wagon because, you remember, then they counted 120 and locked. And this-- this trip was a nightmare. It took forever. They were going-- and this is a journey of 100 kilometers, not more, if even that. And they-- they moved us left, and right, and center, and-- and never opened the wagon. They just went on, and on, and on. So finally, the-- the-- the train stopped, and they opened the wagons. And we were in the middle of a wood. It was very hot, very hot. And, again, alles raus, and we were completely-- we couldn't stand up, you know, after this crouching position.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you received any food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, the wagons were sealed.

speaker: interviewer
text: No food, no water?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was some pail with water. They-- we received some food for the journey. They gave us, maybe, a piece of bread. I would be lying. I don't know. I don't remember. I really don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did everybody survive the trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And from-- from that little station, there was a platform in the middle of the woods. They marched us, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] basically, to the ultimate hell, which was Belsen. And it was the ultimate hell because they did not bury their dead since, I don't know, since Christmas, or I don't know since when. And there was-- they didn't give us anything to eat, and they didn't give us anything to drink. Once every three days, they-- they-- somebody yelled water. And then you had to fight to get a little bit of water, or a little bit of thin gruel. And that was that. And there were dead bodies lying absolutely everywhere. You know, even today, if I close my eyes and look at Belsen to remember Belsen, there is nothing but dead bodies, in every shape and form. We stopped seeing them. People sat on them, defecated on them--
